Transcrição Completa: 24 Unique Mechanism Process Lessons 7 5 25
[00:00]
All right, so weekly inner circle group call time. Today we've got a request from Nick. Nick, I have no idea how to pronounce your last name. I don't think anybody does, you know. Maybe put out one of those YouTube videos where it's like a pronunciation video so the world knows how to say. You know what I'm talking about.
[00:18]
Anyway, Nick was asking questions specifically about personal branding as it relates to having unique mechanisms to stand out. He was giving a set of lessons where he was talking about an issue with - he simply put - he stopped talking about his unique mechanism on YouTube and had lowered his quantity of purchases a lot and as a result of that he learned a powerful lesson.
[00:40]
You got to have a unique mechanism. You got to have a process that you can articulate that helps get people confident in your process about what you can do for them in terms of getting results. And he unfortunately learned that lesson the hard way.
[00:55]
So, in order to ideally prevent all of you guys from learning that lesson the hard way, we're going to teach some of the best practices as it relates to unique mechanisms and having a clear process to result.
[01:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Breakthrough Advertising - Eugene Schwartz"
NÚMEROS: $125 (preço do livro)
VISUAL: Referência ao livro clássico de marketing dos anos 1950, conceito de unique mechanism e market sophistication
[01:10]
So, first of all, for all those unfamiliar, there's an incredible book, probably the most costly book that I think you will purchase that talks about some of these lessons. That book is called Breakthrough Advertising. Breakthrough Advertising is if you buy it from their website directly 125 bucks. You can buy it on Amazon for a little more money if you so choose.
[01:30]
Written in the 1950s by Eugene Schwarz. He talks about this concept called unique mechanism and process as it relates to market sophistication.
[01:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Unique Mechanism Definition"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Definição conceitual - algo específico que você faz e pelo qual se torna conhecido, separando você dos competidores
[01:42]
So for everybody who doesn't know, this is really important to understand this. Unique mechanism is very simple to understand. It's essentially something that you specifically do and become known for that helps separate you from competitors in the market. That's all it really is.
[02:00]
And when it comes to process, your unique mechanism and what you do to help get somebody a result can be articulated in a step-by-step process. And if the person that you're selling to has a bunch of other options and there's a bunch of competitors in the market that they can choose between, then these two different things, having a unique mechanism, and in addition to that, having a process that you openly articulate in your advertising, on your funnels, in your organic content, these are things that people have to believe in in order to drive purchases at scale.
[02:35]
If you fail to have these two things when you're in a high competition market, you have a much higher probability to have more money flow to the competitors comparatively to flowing to you.

[05:28]
Like sure there was still a couple unique ones that existed but like McLaren didn't exist. You know there wasn't a lot of other options at the time that were as popular as what there is today.

[06:08]
If you're sitting in the in the P in the driver's seat and like you're looking out your window, without exaggeration, you have like less than a foot of visibility out of that fucking windshield. Okay, it's thin and it is not a lot of visibility.
[06:25]
Okay, it's got a real cockpit a cockpit like feel as well. In terms of the car, when you go and you look at what it's like to sit inside of one of these things, it's like there's this massive column in the middle of the in the middle of the the center console there.
[06:42]
And this one in specific, this one's a roadster, you know, so it's got the roof that comes off, but like it again, it's got like arguably a very jet-like feel. You know, you sit inside of these things, you can barely see out of the windshield from how angled the windshield is.
[07:00]
The center console's fucking massive. And there's a ton of buttons. There's like I want to say at least like two dozen, maybe three dozen buttons in this thing. There's like switches.
[07:12]

[07:28]
It's like just as an example of a simple unique mechanism, the little starter switch having the red lid and then you press the button, you know, and again to be clear, no matter which Lamborghini you get, whether it's a Urus, whether it's an SVJ, whether it's the Revuelto, you know, whether it's a Scion, doesn't matter which one you get, okay?
[07:50]
They all have the little red switch with the button because that's an example of their unique mechanism.
[07:58]

Like, if you've ever been in a Lamborghini and you pull up to a stoplight, you'll know that you have to like literally lean down and like look up to fucking see that stoplight. [14:40]

[15:00]

[15:40]
That is arguably a unique mechanism as well because although yes, other car manufacturers leverage carbon fiber, they don't have a massive carbon fiber tub. So these cars are extremely light and like you feel that when you're driving.

[15:58]

[16:15]
the driving experience, like the driving experience of a Lamborghini versus a Ferrari versus a McLaren are all also completely different. They drive wildly different.
[16:28]
Like as an example, most of the historical Lamborghinis that have been the most popular, like Aventadors, as an example, they're single clutch. So when you're driving, it's like and then you shift and you get this moment where you kind of like jolt forward and then it kicks into the next gear.
[16:45]
Ferraris have a really fast rev range. They rev to a higher RPM and they also have dual clutch. Same thing with McLaren. So, they shift faster. So, so you have a lot more speed.
[17:00]
McLaren's also feel like they're going to let you die. Okay, this feels like the ultimate supercar in the sense that it's like fully unleashed.
[17:10]

[19:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Frontend vs Backend Unique Mechanisms"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Camadas de mecanismos únicos - frontend (consideração inicial) vs backend (após compra/durante uso)
[19:10]
Now, I can't stress it enough just as a simple thing. It's like that's not something I'm marketing on the front end. That specific unique mechanism is more equivalent to like the driving experience of the car.
[19:22]
You you have to understand that there's that there's layers to unique mechanism. Okay? There's front end unique mechanisms and then there's backend unique mechanisms.
[19:32]
Okay? So, think about this. that as an example is a back-end unique mechanism. The driving experience is a back-end unique mechanism. Okay, these are things that differentiate once somebody's already in the buying process.
[19:48]
When somebody's more so in the consideration process, they consider front-end unique mechanisms.
[19:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cody Sanchez Business Model Example"
NÚMEROS: 5+ competidores mencionados em média nas calls de vendas, 2-3 meses de mudança algorítmica
VISUAL: Exemplo de mercado competitivo - ensinar compra de negócios, múltiplos competidores surgindo, diferenciação através de mecanismo único
[19:58]
So, let me give you an example with one of my clients. So, when we look at Cody Sanchez's business model, she specifically teaches how to buy a business. Okay. And she's owned that market for a very long time. Arguably the biggest by far.
[20:15]
Has she had competitors? Yeah. Yes, she has, for sure. Have those competitors ever actually meant anything or made a difference or even gotten brought up on calls? No. No. Surprisingly, no.
[20:30]
It's actually been the exact opposite for several years. Like, it's very rare, as a matter of fact, when somebody brings up a competitor. But most recently, after that algorithm update on Facebook, where after you click on an ad or show any relative interest to something, they serve every single fucking competitor under the sun for you immediately following that interest.
[20:52]
So, moral of the story, on the most recent, like I'd say two almost three months ongoing now, the sales people are bringing up the fact that upwards of five plus people are are showing up to call.
[21:08]
like they're showing up to calls and they're they're bringing up like five plus competitors. Like the average sales call now is actively saying, "Oh, I'm also considering, you know, at least three people, an average of five people, sometimes even more than that."
[21:25]
And the only real things that the customer at that stage in the process asks about is unique mechanism differences.
[21:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Competitor Unique Mechanism Comparison"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Cody: curso + comunidade vs Competidor: sourcing deal + funding + scaling (done-with-you), diferenças de preço e entrega
[21:35]
So these other competitors that she has, first of all, one of the main differences can be price. So when you look at front-end unique mechanism differences, price definitely makes a difference. Okay, that's definitely one of the things, but it's also how you go about delivering.
[21:52]
Okay, so how you deliver. So in this case for her, all she does is teach people how to buy a business. She has a course and she has a community.
[22:02]
Some of these other competitors, their unique mechanisms are we will source a deal for you, line up the funding, and help you scale the business after you acquire it. That's completely different than just teaching somebody how to buy a business and then putting them in a community.
[22:20]
That's more of a done with you offer, arguably a done for you offer to a degree, but there's a lot of stuff the other person needs to do, so we'll classify it as a done with you offer.
[22:32]
Think about just those differences alone. The unique mechanism in this case is course and community. The unique mechanism that they're comparing it against is this other guy is going to go and fucking find a deal for me, line up the funding, and help me scale it after I buy it.
[22:50]
The price is higher, but to be clear, that's a big difference, a unique mechanism, right?
[22:58]
And so in this particular case, has there been other people that have had the exact same unique mechanism historically? Yeah. Oh, I'll teach you how to buy a business. I'll put you in a course in community.
[23:10]
You know, nobody's really given a fuck about those other people because again, it's the same unique mechanism. Cody comes off as more credible. She spends more money to reach customers first before the other people do.
[23:25]
There's there's never really been an instance where somebody's brought up a competitor who does the exact same thing. The only time that they bring up something different is when there's actually something different that a competitor does. Really think about this.

[27:12]

[29:25]

[29:48]
Okay? And then there's broader reasons like there's reasons that are at these outer layers of the circle where people are going to purchase at a higher rate because of these specific things.
[30:02]

[47:10]

[47:25]
Okay, I look at the cars. I get a car that I like. So, let's say that we wanted to buy this McLaren Senna right here for 1.3 mil. Okay, before I even do anything else, obviously, I'm going to sell myself on the car, right?
[47:42]
We're going to look at the Carfax, right? We're going to see if there's any issues with the vehicle. In this case, it's had two owners. It's got a good service history. In this case, it looks like a very extensive service history. That's good to see.
[48:00]
Looks like it's out of warranty. That's a little shaky. I would have a question if it has an extended warranty. It's got no accidents reported. That's tremendous.
[48:12]

[48:28]

[50:10]

[50:30]

[50:48]
Okay? I don't go to dealers to buy cars. It's not how I've bought any car literally ever. Every dealer I bought cars from, I buy it fully remote without even having to go in person.
[51:02]
I send somebody else to go in person and do a professional inspection on the car. Once they give me the approval, I have a funding company we work with. They pay for the car. I pay the funding company. Sign some paperwork. Send a tow guy over. Car shows up at my crib.

[51:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Carvana Case Study"
NÚMEROS: 2012 (ano de lançamento)
VISUAL: Carvana marketing process como unique mechanism - compra de carro sem ir à concessionária, revolucionário em 2012
[51:45]
So, Carvana, right? Regular person car site. Their whole shtick that made them very popular and this is really important to understand. I bring this up as a specific example because they marketed their process as their unique mechanism.
[52:02]
Okay. So, Carvana's process is very simple. You get to go on here. You get to look at a bunch of cars. You input your information. All the prices you see right here are the amount that you specifically would have to put down that you specifically would pay a month and the price specifically for you based on your credit profile.
[52:22]
And you sit here and let's say we wanted to buy this shitty little Jeep right here. Okay. From here, it's like this is called a virtual showroom. So, I can sit here and I can spin the car around.
[52:35]
They tell me anything that's wrong with it. These with these little tiny blue things, and they also highlight good things about the car with the little blue things. We can go into the interior of the vehicle with the press of a button.
[52:50]
And we can do the same thing on the inside and look around the whole stick here. And we also obviously just have standard photos that we can go and look at for all these vehicles down below.
[53:02]
They show you how many keys come with it. They show you how fast they can deliver it or or they tell you you can come and pick it up. They give you the Carfax and all that. They tell you a bunch of features, all the details of the vehicle and then they talk about the funding.
[53:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pre-2012 Car Buying Experience"
NÚMEROS: Horas (tempo médio na concessionária), dia inteiro necessário
VISUAL: Experiência antiga - ir fisicamente à concessionária, esperar horas, perder dia inteiro de trabalho
[53:20]
Now, to be clear, Carvana again, their whole stick was we are going to help you buy a car without you having to ever come into the dealership. when Carvana originally came out, right?
[53:35]
Carvana started 2012. Who remembers how it was like to buy a car prior to 2012? Every car I bought before then when I was in high school, in this case, I'd watch my parents buy cars. They'd have to go into the dealer and they'd have to sit there.
[53:52]
Who remembers all that shit You guys remember that? How long on average did it take to sit in a dealer to be able to buy a car? It's like hours on end. That's all I ever hold my parents and other people who bought cars bitch about.
[54:08]
Was like, "Oh, dude, I got to go to the fucking car dealership to be able to buy a car. It's going to take forever." You know, they have to set aside a whole fucking day to do it. They have to take off work or go buy it on a weekend.
[54:22]
You know, hours and hours and hours unnecessary like you didn't just have to spend money. You had to spend time, too.
[54:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Carvana's Market Disruption"
NÚMEROS: 2012 até hoje
VISUAL: Carvana como primeiro a fazer compra remota de carros, processo como unique mechanism em indústria altamente competitiva
[54:35]
My whole experience in my adult life has been the exact opposite. It's been that of convenience. It's been the Carvana model. But Carvana was the first to do it.
[54:48]
So Carvana, their unique mechanism was their process. Okay? Through marketing their process, they became who we all know them as today. And there's arguably the highest competition industry that exists. One of them anyway is selling cars.
[55:08]
So think about this in these later stage markets, okay? You have to become a master of process. And process is really simple to understand. Process is just the steps to result spoken out loud.
[55:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Inner Circle Process Example"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Processo articulado: juntar ao grupo → cursos → AI Jeremy + Real Jeremy → masterminds → networking → SOPs → accountability
[55:28]
Okay? So it's just openly saying. So as an example, let's correlate it here to the inner circle. We constantly tell people, we're like, "Yeah, so in order for us to help you make a lot more money, it's pretty simple process.
[55:42]
You're going to join into the group. We're going to add you into a fuckload of courses. We're going to assign some specific videos that we want you to watch. You can start talking to AI Jeremy and real Jeremy on them immediately with some of the problems that you have.
[56:00]
That way, we can help get you an immediate result. Make sure you come to one of the most recent masterminds that's, you know, soon enough. Either maybe the one coming up or one of the one of the next ones.
[56:15]

[56:28]

[56:42]
And from there, we hold you accountable to actually do all this stuff. We just don't tolerate people staying in the same spot. We're going to hold you accountable to actually getting this shit done.
[56:55]
Okay, now this is important to understand this. Very important you understand this. That's our process. Like that's just us articulating the steps to get a result.
[57:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Layers of Results"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Problemas imediatos vs problemas evolutivos, pensamento de curto prazo vs longo prazo, múltiplas fases de crescimento
[57:10]
And it's like now there's layers to results. Okay. One thing that happens with very shortsighted people in this very group, they have a very specific pressing problem and they think that's all they need to solve. And that's an impoverished way of thinking.
[57:28]
It's obviously not even remotely close to accurate way to think because there's always another revenue driven action that they could take to make more money. And it's like the problems evolve as you evolve and overcome the specific challenges you face.
[57:45]
So, like, you know, once you get your show rate up or once you get your close rate up or, you know, once you get that funding part that you need to get more deals closed or whatever the specific problem is, whatever the immediate result is that you think you need to focus on most,
[58:02]
once you get that result, that's exciting because then there's a bunch of other shit that we can teach you immediately after. And we make sure to articulate that because we're aware of what specific I guess the phases that people want to go through when they get results with us.
[58:20]
They want the immediate thing that's most pressing to them handled and then they want our help to hold their hand to the next levels of the game which typically involves a bunch of scaling lessons.
[58:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Process Articulation in High Competition"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[58:38]

[58:48]
So think about this, okay? When we look at Cody Sanchez's business and we say she has a course in a community and then there's new competitors that have said, "I'll help you buy the business. I'll find you a deal to buy. I'll help fund the deal to buy it and then I'll help scale up the business once you buy it.
[59:08]
It's like, well, now we can no longer just say, yeah, we're going to put you in a course in a community about how to buy a business. We have to openly articulate the process and sell why that is better than that other unique mechanism that now we have to sell against.
[59:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cody Sanchez Process Defense"

[59:30]
So, we don't necessarily have to change the unique mechanism. We have to articulate the process to result and make it known why that makes more sense.
[59:42]

Why would that business that's going to help you buy a business, why in the world would they give you the best deals?
[1:00:02]
They're they're just going to buy them themselves. Doesn't make any sense to give you the best deals. And if you're not trained on how to buy a business, you're not going to know if you're getting the best business that they're serving to you.
[1:00:18]
You don't even know if you're going to get good funding because you don't know about how to get funding to buy a business. You might not even need funding to buy the business.
[1:00:28]

[1:00:42]
They just leverage their way in there from a set of skills they have and a and a a person that they're buying the business from is in a position where they might want to retire and they need your skills to continue running the business.
[1:00:58]
So, they'll happily give you a huge equitable percentage to run it and you just give that business owner a revshare. You know, you help them fund their retirement.
[1:01:10]
It's like, so if you don't know that, right, like you're just going to go into extreme debt to buy a business that might not even be a good business to buy.
[1:01:22]
So, what we're going to do is we're going to teach you how to buy businesses, how to analyze deals, all the different ways you can fund a deal, both creative and with hard money. And then we're going to teach you how to scale the business, too, of course.
[1:01:38]

[1:11:38]

[1:11:48]
So for the podcast thing you're talking about, you mentioned a few of them in your in your your chat response there. [1:12:12]

[1:12:28]

[1:12:45]

[1:13:08]

[1:20:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Bundling vs Separation Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Sistema de 10 passos (Cody), 4 backend selling systems
VISUAL: Quando agrupar tudo sob um nome vs quando manter separado, compartmentalização cerebral
[1:20:18]
Yes, she actually has a 10-step process for how she compartmentalizes all of her all of her course lessons to be clear, but she still does the same thing. Like she has like unique systems that she deploys that are a part of that 10-step overall system that she names specifically.
[1:20:38]
So, it's still a best practice regardless. to be clear, like there's there's differences between when you need to bundle everything together under a specific name and when you have like a set of things that need to be labeled and bundled together.
[1:20:55]
So like as an example, like the backend selling systems, those are currently four specific things that deserve to be compartmentalized together, but they also all have unique names in themselves. You know, they're completely different than front-end selling systems.
[1:21:12]
Now, if I put all the lessons of front-end selling systems with back-end selling systems, to your point, I just have to name it something, you know, and I could name it a system or a process or, you know, whatever.
[1:21:28]
But the the point is is like sometimes you don't really have to, you know, sometimes it's just better for playing into how our brains work with compartmentalization to like name and bundle specific things, but you don't always have to go like to the top and like, you know, put an umbrella over literally everything.
[1:21:48]
You know, sometimes that separation can actually be good. So for me, as an example, with my education company, playing into that logic of well, there's so many things to learn and there's so many things to do. It's like I would have want to bundle it into a simple five steps.
[1:22:08]
I think I would actually do a disservice to like the amount of attention some of these things independently deserve. You know, I think it makes sense separated in that example to truly demonstrate the fact that there is so much to learn and there is so much to do. Does that make sense?
[1:22:28]
Yeah. Yeah. Thank you.
[1:22:32]
Okay, cool. Other questions?

[1:24:30]

You know, let's say once you have somebody's attention and let's say they're comparing you to other agencies and stuff, what do you think in your mind before they talk to you convinces them the most that you're the one to go with before you talk to them?
[1:24:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pre-Call Conviction Factors"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[1:24:55]

I'm not sitting here saying everybody knows who I am to be clear, but moral of the story is is like you can generally ask somebody like, "Hey, do you have any experience with this Jeremy Haynes guy?"
[1:25:22]
And believe it or not, it's either a high probability that individual does if they're in our ideal target demographic or somebody that they're like one degree of separation away from has had as an experience. And so like word of mouth can get around very easily in that example.

[1:25:42]

[1:25:58]

[1:26:12]
Like I'll pick a specific content ad strategy. We'll pitch them on the tornado as an example. So what is initially going to get some of the okay I'm willing to talk to this guy that level of interest is established because of the unique mechanism that we've articulated to them
[1:26:32]
and in most instances we've also soft flexed a specific individual or set of individuals that's highly probable to be known that we've done that unique mechanism with and gotten results for.
[1:26:48]
So when you look at our specific agency as an example and when we do our video pitches as a perfect use case, they have no idea who we are. We're pitching them on a strategy that they believe in, which is a unique mechanism and somebody that we've done it for that's gotten a result that they might know that they might be able to go ask about. Does that make sense?
[1:27:12]
That makes sense. Perfect. Thank you.
[1:27:18]

[1:27:32]

[1:27:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Additional Study Examples"
NÚMEROS: $1 margaritas (Applebee's)
VISUAL: Exemplos para estudar: Applebee's vs Chili's vs Red Lobster, burgers (smash patties, wagyu, sauces, buns), energy drinks, protein powders, green powders, nootropics
[1:27:42]
Yeah. So, what I would do first of all, and this is this is really cool when you go look in the market from this perspective is go out there and try to find specific examples cuz it's super obvious.
[1:27:58]
Like if you look at Applebee's versus Chili's, it's like even just that subtle example to a lot of people from the outside in, they're damn near the exact same business.
[1:28:12]
But like when you actually look at some of the specific unique mechanisms that they have, it's like Applebee's will occasionally do the $1 margaritas, right? Applebee's is more like classic American frozen food. Chili's is more like classic Mexican frozen food is how they differentiate themselves.
[1:28:32]
Red Lobster is kind of in that category now as well. Red Lobster is the seafood variation of that same model. You understand?
[1:28:42]
It's like think about burgers as an example of unique mechanisms. Some of them do smash patties, some of them do wagyu, some of them do like quarter pound beef patties, you know, some of them do like specific sauce combinations. Some of them do different buns that are, you know, again, combinations of these different things that I just discussed.
[1:29:05]
Once you officially become aware, like Nick said, energy drinks absolutely. Protein powders, pre-workouts, also a really good example. I'd also argue the green powders, the green powders and some of those supplement brands that are out there.
[1:29:22]
Like if you go look at Nootropics as a good example of this, specifically the big ones that compete against each other. there's there's a lot of like very key differences.
[1:29:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Onnit Brand Example"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Onnit (Joe Rogan) - único diferenciador é o endorsement, outros vendem mais barato sem pagar celebrity
[1:29:38]
I always think of the the brand that Joe Rogan has, the Onnit brand. I truly think the only differentiator in their unique mechanism is that they have Joe Rogan that endorses it.
[1:29:52]
I think there's plenty of other supplements that sell the exact same thing technically cheaper because they don't have to pay Joe Rogan and that becomes their unique mechanism.
[1:30:05]
You know, like there's so many different cool examples out there that you can see in the wild when you become aware of of unique mechanisms specifically and then you also look at how people are marketing their processes.
[1:30:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Process Marketing Examples"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Carvana (processo), Benihana (cozinhar na frente = processo/unique mechanism)
[1:30:25]
I gave you the example of Carvana when it comes to marketing processes, but another good one that I mentioned was restaurants. Like look at places like Benihana, right?
[1:30:38]
Like Benihana's unique mechanism is that they cook the food right in front of you. But again, that could also be marketed as a process. You know what I mean? Like you go there, you sit down, they they cook the food in front of you. You know, they serve it up like in batches.
[1:30:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Three Main Drivers of Purchase"
NÚMEROS: 3 principais drivers
VISUAL: Unique Mechanism + Price + Process = principais razões de compra após fase de consideração
[1:31:00]
You know, the point I'm trying to make is a lot of buying motives, like the reasons that people purchase after they're in the consideration phase and like ready to actually consider purchasing almost always come down to unique mechanism, price, and process.
[1:31:22]
The main three drivers of why people actually buy stuff once they're in the position of like, I want to buy something.
[1:31:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "When to Use These Lessons"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Não frontend leading hook, mas prevalente em: landing pages, VSLs, sales calls, confirmation videos, emails, hammer content
[1:31:35]
This lesson, I can't stress it enough, is not necessarily like a front-end, you know, this is what you want to lead with type communication. This is something that you want to make very prevalent inside of like landing pages, VSSLs, sales calls, back-end selling content like confirmation page videos, value dense emails, hammer them content.
[1:32:00]
You absolutely can market these things on the front end in ads, but they aren't necessarily like your leading hook unless you're communicating to in-market buyers. In-market buyers, you can use all these things as front-end tactics to be very direct with you.
[1:32:20]
So, just simply put, be very mindful of these things as lessons because when you use them successfully, especially in high competition markets, you have a much higher probability to get buyers than you did prior to that.
[1:32:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Later Stage Market Adaptation"
NÚMEROS: Anos (duração de vantagem de Cody)
VISUAL: Empresas que sofrem mais = falham em adaptar messaging quando chegam mercados de estágio avançado
[1:32:40]
Generally the businesses that suffer most are the ones who fail to adapt to this specific set of messaging once they get into later later stage markets.
[1:32:55]
Most people can again in less lower competition environments. They can get away with just making claims, spending more money than everybody else like I gave you the example Cody was able to do for years.
[1:33:12]
But then when the competition officially comes, you no longer get the luxury of being able to ignore these principles. like you literally have to execute and follow them otherwise you're going to lose a lot of money.
[1:33:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Nick's Case Study Request"
NÚMEROS: Milhares de views (mainstream content), 1000 views + 5 discovery calls (unique mechanism content)
VISUAL: Comparação: parar unique mechanism = queda massiva em vendas, voltar = aumento imediato
[1:33:32]
Nick, do you want to do you want to articulate by the way since you were the one who asked the question what happened to you when you stopped doing your unique mechanism?
[1:33:45]
Yeah. So my unique mechanism is all about just like marketing fundamentals and that's like my process how I go out there do the research come up with the ad angles that we create for those ad accounts and showing that on YouTube videos almost every sale they're like yeah Nick we'd love your process of how you go about coming up with the ads and stuff like that.
[1:34:10]
I started getting grasses greener on the other side and started going after some of this other like content other ecom YouTubers were making. And as soon as I started just creating like various things like here's how beginner beginner way to run Facebook ads, here's like an easy scaling strategy on Facebook ads, just random shit that wasn't related to marketing fundamentals.
[1:34:35]
I've seen a huge decrease in students, huge decrease in leads for the agency and stuff in my YouTube content. And like I kid you not, literally going back and started putting content back to like what I originally was doing, like immediate increase students, discovery calls for agency, all of those things right there.
[1:34:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Key Lesson from Nick's Experience"
NÚMEROS: 1 vídeo = 1000 views = 5 discovery calls (multi-7-figure brands) vs 100K views = 0 calls
VISUAL: Mercado valoriza você pelos unique mechanisms específicos, não conteúdo genérico
[1:35:00]
That's a great lesson. You know, you're viewed by the market as valuable for what you specifically mentioned your unique mechanisms are. And although I'm sure people appreciate what you're communicating to be fair, that didn't drive buying intent.
[1:35:22]
Yeah, that was a big one. Like I said, with the with the like the Lamborghini windshield being so low, it's like that's definitely unique to Lamborghinis, but it's not the reason that people are going to buy them, you know?
[1:35:38]
It's like if Lamborghini went all in on marketing how angled their fucking windshields are, like they would lose a lot of money because that's not the unique mechanism the market gives a fuck about.
[1:35:52]
So, Nick, I got a question for you, Nick, if that's cool, Jeremy. Yeah, go ahead. What you got?
[1:36:00]
So, so you're saying you went like more mainstream in your YouTube content strategy to attract like more viewers and it just fucked you?
[1:36:12]
Yeah. Like I had a video I posted last week that got literally like a thousand views and had like five discovery calls booked from it with like large multi-seven-figure ecom brands.
[1:36:28]
whereas I've had a I've had like multiple videos now that were mainstream Facebook ad content that's gotten like one video got 100,000 views. Couple others got like three 4,000 views that I can't attribute back a single sales call or single student to.
[1:36:48]
Wow. Yeah. That's what I experienced too. I hired a new YouTube strategist and he was like, "Oh, this is how we're going to do it." He had the whole funnel set up in his mind of top of funnel, middle of funnel, bottom of funnel, and it it just fucked everything.
[1:37:08]
and same exact thing happened. I was like, I don't care about views. All I care about is buyers. But all these YouTube guys, they care about views because, you know, when they're on their their their call, that's what they're going to be presented to you.
[1:37:25]
Yeah. I just hired a Instagram strategist and a Twitter ghost writer, and I'm having that same conversation with all the sales people right now because they're getting a lot of views, but very few discovery calls. So, now we're transitioning back to my unique process on those two platforms.
[1:37:48]
Yeah. Damn. Yep. It's how it goes.

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$125 (preço do livro Breakthrough Advertising)
$1.3 milhões (McLaren Senna exemplo)
$2-3 milhões (Paganis na Prestige Imports)
$700 milhões (contrato Shohei Ohtani)
41 pessoas ajudadas a alcançar $1M/mês
100,000 views (vídeo mainstream sem conversão)
1,000 views (vídeo de unique mechanism com 5 discovery calls)
5+ competidores mencionados em média nas sales calls
2-3 meses de mudança algorítmica do Facebook
2012 (lançamento Carvana)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Unique Mechanism: Algo específico que você faz e pelo qual se torna conhecido, separando você dos competidores
Process: Passos para resultado articulados claramente (steps to result spoken out loud)
Content-First Ad Strategies: Venus Fly Trap + dúzias de outras estratégias
Backend Selling Systems: Show rates, close rates, pay in fulls, sales cycle timelines
Video Pitch Strategy: Articular estratégia única + soft flex de cliente conhecido com resultados
Carvana Model: Compra remota de veículos sem ir à concessionária (processo como unique mechanism)
Marketing Fundamentals: Processo de pesquisa e criação de ângulos de anúncios (Nick's unique mechanism)
ESTRUTURA:
Frontend vs Backend Unique Mechanisms:
Frontend: Consideração inicial, usado em landing pages, VSLs, ads
Backend: Experiência pós-compra, driving experience, relacionamento
Bullseye Model: Camadas de unique mechanisms - centro (razões pequenas mas precisas) vs exterior (razões amplas)
Spoken vs Unspoken Knowns: Verdades não faladas que precisam ser posicionadas mas não verbalizadas
Three Main Purchase Drivers: Unique Mechanism + Price + Process
Market Sophistication Stages: Mercados early stage vs late stage (alta competição)
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Luxury Lease Partners (LLP) para financiamento de supercars
Cody Sanchez: curso + comunidade vs competidores done-with-you (sourcing + funding + scaling)
Inner Circle: 1-on-1 calls + weekly group calls + quarterly masterminds + massive course library
Membros do Inner Circle: $1M/mês até $5M/mês
EXEMPLOS DE UNIQUE MECHANISMS POR INDÚSTRIA:
Supercars:
Lamborghini: Para-brisa extremamente inclinado (<1 pé visibilidade), cockpit tipo jato, 24-36 botões, botão ignição com tampa vermelha, portas scissor, exhaust extremamente alto
Ferrari: Para-brisa normal, Ferrari Red/Yellow, motor central, herança de corrida, prestígio clássico
McLaren: Design UFO-like arredondado, portas dihedral, asa móvel/airbrake 90°, carbon fiber monocoque, interior espaçoso, exhaust mais quieto
Esportes:
Ja Morant: Comemorações únicas (gun motions, grenade celebration)
LeBron James: Força física, dunks devastadores
Shohei Ohtani: Personalidade agradável, gambling, home runs, roubo de bases
Restaurantes:
Applebee's: $1 margaritas, comida americana congelada clássica
Chili's: Comida mexicana congelada clássica
Red Lobster: Variação seafood
Benihana: Cozinhar na frente dos clientes (processo como unique mechanism)
Jeremy Haynes Unique Mechanisms:
Teaching style (direto, sem BS, agressivo quando necessário)
Content ad strategies (Venus Fly Trap + dúzias de outras)
Backend selling systems (4 sistemas específicos)
Ajudar pessoas a transformar dinheiro em mais dinheiro com certeza
41 pessoas escaladas a $1M/mês
1-on-1 calls + weekly calls + quarterly masterminds
Mix de membros high-level ($1M-5M/mês) + técnicos
DURAÇÃO: ~1h 38min
FORMATO: Weekly Inner Circle Group Call - Aula sobre Unique Mechanisms e Process
